growth_records.strain: not stated in text (model Roseobacter-group marine bacterium; metabolic model per ref. 33)
growth_records.agit: 130 rpm (preculture); intermittent shaking in plate reader
growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater medium ASWb with 1 mM glucose

up.method: sole carbon source (1 mM); consumption not measured as a flux

mfa.note: 50 uM [13C-methyl]DMSP, 2 h; label traced into methionine cycle. Isotope abundance, NOT a flux
Same experiment; MTA is spermidine-synthesis byproduct downstream of SAM

growth_records.cite: Sperfeld, Martin et al. Algal methylated compounds shorten the lag phase of Phaeobacter inhibens bacteria. Nature Microbiology (2024). DOI:10.1038/s41564-024-01742-6. [PMC11306105]
growth_records.method: reported (Growthcurver logistic fit of OD600 growth curves yields modelled growth rate, lag time and doubling time; numeric mu/td only in figures/source data, not in article text). DMSP supplementation shortened lag by up to 2.5 h without changing doubling time
growth_records.snippet: doubling times were unchanged, as evident from the identical slopes, while lag times were shortened ... 13C-labelled SAM (isotope abundance for M+1: 41.6%) and 13C-labelled MTA (isotope abundance for M+1: 47.9%)
growth_records.notes: Defined-medium (ASWb, full salt composition captured above) batch growth of the prokaryote P. inhibens on 1 mM glucose, 30 C, aerobic. Growth quantified by Growthcurver (mu, lag, doubling time) but numeric mu/td are in source-data/figures, not article text, so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h left null. No mmol/gDW/h substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates reported. 13C-methyl DMSP labelling of SAM (41.6%) and MTA (47.9%) are isotope abundances (M+1), not MFA fluxes. Cellular C1 requirement estimate (190.1 amol C1 and 23,800 amol ATP per cell duplication) is a model-based calculation adapted from E. coli (ref. 32) and the P. inhibens metabolic model (ref. 33), not a measured rate. [metadata completed from paper Methods: pH]

growth_records.strain: not stated (Roseobacter group; experimental data from Wünsch et al. 2021)
growth_records.medium: defined mixture of 11 carbon sources (3 sugars + 8 amino acids)

up.units: per h (specific uptake rate); effectively blocked while inhibitory sugars persist
up.method: DEB model fit to Wünsch et al. 2021 depletion curves; glucose uptake inhibited by N-acetylglucosamine and/or mannitol

yields.note: substrate energy yields and theoretical ATP yields per catabolized molecule taken from Wünsch et al. 2021 (SI Table S1); N-acetylglucosamine highest ATP yield and growth rate, mannitol highest growth efficiency, glucose lowest on all indicators

growth_records.cite: Lücken, Leonhard et al. A network-based analysis of bacterial growth on substrate mixtures uncovers glucose inhibition in Phaeobacter inhibens. Scientific Reports (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41598-025-33583-6. [PMC12791127]
growth_records.method: model-fitted (DEB model); experimental CDW/substrate-depletion data originate from Wünsch et al. 2021
growth_records.snippet: P. inhibens continuously consumed all amino acids (initially 1.3 mM each) without diauxic shifts or inhibition patterns ... mannitol and N-acetylglucosamine were depleted much faster than the other substrates ... effectively blocking signif
growth_records.notes: This is a MODELING/re-analysis paper; the experimental growth and substrate-depletion data (batch, marine P. inhibens on 11-substrate mixture) are from Wünsch et al. 2021 (ref 21), NOT measured here. The only in-text quantitative experimental anchor is that each of the 8 amino acids was initially 1.3 mM. Reported growth rates, bacterial growth efficiency (BGE), ATP yields, uptake and half-saturation constants are DEB-model-fitted parameters (is_experimental=false). Key qualitative finding: sequential sugar usage (mannitol & N-acetylglucosamine consumed faster than glucose; glucose uptake inhibited by mannitol/N-acetylglucosamine, i.e., reverse of classic glucose catabolite repression) with simultaneous co-utilization of all amino acids. No numeric experimental mu, uptake or secretion rates are reported in the main text of this paper; original single-substrate and mixture data are in Wünsch et al. 2021. Record retained at low confidence to document the condition and its cited data source.
